stem: An 8-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department with 3 days of coryza and an increasing wheeze. He has taken 60% of his usual feeds over the past 12 hours but is still having normal wet nappies. There is no fever and no history of choking. He was born at 38 weeks gestation and has had three previous episodes of viral wheeze. His father has asthma. On arrival he is alert and pink, with mild subcostal recession and a wet productive cough between feeds.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142, RR 48, SpO2 91% room air, Temp 36.9
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession; bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and expiratory wheeze; CRT 2s; alert
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab pending; chest X-ray not performed

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Admit for supplemental oxygen targeting SpO2 92 to 96% with small-volume oral or nasogastric feeds
options.rationale: AU paediatric guidance is to admit infants with bronchiolitis when SpO2 is below 92% in room air or feeding is below 50% of normal; oxygen is titrated to maintain SpO2 92 to 96% and hydration is supported by nasogastric or small-volume oral feeds. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg and reassess clinical response over the next 30 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in infants under 12 months with bronchiolitis as the wheeze reflects small-airway oedema rather than smooth-muscle bronchospasm; trials show no meaningful response. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence inhaled fluticasone via spacer and review with the general practitioner in 48 hours
options.rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids have no role in acute bronchiolitis and do not modify illness duration; a primary-care review at 48 hours would also miss the existing hypoxia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Administer nebulised hypertonic 3% saline three times daily plus a short course of oral prednisolone
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline shows inconsistent benefit and is not part of routine AU paediatric care; oral corticosteroids do not alter outcomes in viral bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with oral amoxicillin to cover likely lower respiratory bacterial co-infection
options.rationale: Routine antibiotics are not indicated as bronchiolitis is viral; bacterial co-infection rates are low and discharge with hypoxia is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is the commonest cause of hospitalisation in infants under 12 months and is managed almost entirely with supportive care. AU paediatric guidance frames admission decisions around hypoxia, feeding adequacy, and work of breathing rather than imaging or laboratory testing. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis because the underlying pathology is mucosal oedema and debris rather than bronchospasm or bacterial infection. The shift in oxygen target from 94% to 92% in recent AU guidelines was driven by evidence that the lower threshold reduces length of stay without increasing safety events.
key_points: SpO2 92% in room air is the AU admission threshold for bronchiolitis
Feeding under 50% of usual or significant work of breathing also mandates admission
Bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics have no routine role
Nasogastric feeds and minimal handling are preferred over IV fluids when tolerated
explanation.pearls: RCH CPG sets bronchiolitis oxygen target at 92 to 96% and admits feeds below 50% of normal; bronchodilator trials add no value under 12 months.

stem: A 2-year-old boy presents to a regional emergency department in the early hours with a barking cough and inspiratory stridor that wakes him from sleep. The cough started 24 hours earlier with rhinorrhoea and a low-grade fever. He has otherwise been well, is fully vaccinated, and has had no choking or drooling. While in the cubicle he is settled on his mother's lap, taking sips of juice with no audible stridor at rest, and his voice sounds hoarse but normal in volume. He has had one previous episode 6 months ago which resolved without treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Barking cough on examination; no stridor at rest; mild intermittent suprasternal tug only when crying; air entry normal bilaterally

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management at this point?

options.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and prepare for paediatric retrieval team transfer
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for moderate to severe croup with stridor and recession at rest; this child is mild with no rest stridor. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Give a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg per kg and observe for at least 30 minutes before discharge
options.rationale: A single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg per kg is recommended for all severities of croup, including mild, and reduces re-presentation; observation of around 30 minutes before discharge confirms stability. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Reassure the parents and discharge home with humidified air advice and paracetamol as needed
options.rationale: Humidified air has no evidence base in croup; discharge without dexamethasone misses an opportunity to reduce illness duration and re-presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Take a lateral neck radiograph to exclude epiglottitis before initiating any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: Imaging is not required to diagnose croup or exclude epiglottitis in a settled child; the clinical picture is diagnostic and imaging may distress and decompensate the airway. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin and review with the general practitioner the following morning for re-examination
options.rationale: Croup is viral and antibiotics are not indicated; this also omits dexamethasone which is the only proven therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Croup is a clinical diagnosis based on the barking cough and stridor in a young child following a viral prodrome. AU paediatric guidance recommends a single dose of oral dexamethasone for all severities, including mild, because it reduces symptom duration, return visits and need for adrenaline. Nebulised adrenaline is added for moderate to severe disease, defined by stridor and recession at rest. Imaging is not required and may worsen distress.
key_points: Dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg per kg orally is given for all severity grades of croup
Mild croup is defined by no stridor and no recession at rest
Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for moderate/severe croup or impending respiratory failure
Antibiotics have no role unless there is suspicion of bacterial tracheitis
explanation.pearls: AU paeds dose of dexamethasone in croup is 0.15 mg per kg orally as a single dose; observe 30 minutes before discharge if mild.

stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to her general practitioner with 24 hours of right-sided ear pain following a recent viral upper respiratory tract infection. She is non-Aboriginal, was breastfed for 6 months, and lives with parents who do not smoke. She has been more irritable but is drinking well and ate her dinner. She has had one previous episode of otitis media 14 months ago which resolved spontaneously. On otoscopy the right tympanic membrane is red, opaque, and bulging with poor mobility; the left is normal. There is no otorrhoea.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 38.1
data_table.Examination: Right TM erythematous, bulging, opaque; no perforation; no mastoid tenderness; alert and playful in waiting room

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic management decision?

options.text: Prescribe immediate oral amoxicillin 30 mg per kg twice daily for 5 days due to the bulging tympanic membrane
options.rationale: Immediate antibiotics are indicated under 6 months or in higher-risk groups; in a well low-risk child over 2 years a watchful waiting strategy is recommended even when the membrane is bulging. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence a delayed prescription of oral cefalexin to be filled if symptoms worsen within the next 6 hours
options.rationale: Cefalexin has poor middle-ear penetration and is not a recommended first-line agent for otitis media; the timeframe also undercuts watchful waiting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Provide analgesia with paracetamol or ibuprofen and review in 48 hours, with antibiotics if no improvement
options.rationale: Analgesia and watchful waiting for 48 hours is recommended in low-risk children over 2 years without high-risk features; antibiotics are added only if symptoms persist or worsen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer the child to an ear nose and throat surgeon for consideration of ventilation tube insertion immediately
options.rationale: Surgical referral applies to recurrent or chronic otitis media with effusion and hearing loss, not a first significant episode in 14 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Otitis Media)

options.text: Prescribe oral co-trimoxazole as covers respiratory pathogens including resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole is not first-line for otitis media in Australia and has rising resistance among pneumococcal isolates; amoxicillin is preferred when antibiotics are indicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media in well children is largely self-limiting and AU antibiotic guidance favours analgesia plus watchful waiting unless high-risk features are present. Immediate antibiotics are warranted under 6 months of age, in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children at risk of chronic suppurative otitis media, with bilateral disease under 2 years, otorrhoea, or systemic toxicity. The first-line oral antibiotic when needed is amoxicillin at high dose; second-line for true type-I penicillin allergy is azithromycin. Symptom resolution at 48 to 72 hours is the trigger to add or escalate therapy.
key_points: Watchful waiting is appropriate for low-risk children over 2 years without otorrhoea or systemic features
Immediate antibiotics for under 6 months, bilateral disease under 2 years, perforation/otorrhoea, or Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children at risk
Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg twice daily is first line when antibiotics are given
Analgesia is the most important short-term intervention
explanation.pearls: AMH and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic endorse 48-hour watchful waiting in low-risk children over 2 years even with a bulging tympanic membrane.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is admitted with 6 days of fever to 39.5, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, a polymorphous truncal rash, cracked red lips, a strawberry tongue, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy of 1.8 cm. He received intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g per kg over 12 hours and oral aspirin 5 mg per kg per day starting 36 hours ago. Despite this, his temperature has remained above 38 and his C-reactive protein has not improved on repeat testing. An echocardiogram performed before treatment showed mild dilation of the proximal left main coronary artery. He has no known immunodeficiency.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 26, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 38.7 (post-IVIG)
data_table.Examination: Persistent conjunctival injection; cracked lips; rash fading; cervical node 1.5 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Continue current aspirin dose and reassess clinically in 48 hours before considering further therapy
options.rationale: Persistent fever and CRP at 36 hours after IVIG defines IVIG resistance in Kawasaki disease and warrants prompt second-line therapy as coronary aneurysm risk rises. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Switch aspirin from anti-inflammatory to antiplatelet dosing and continue observation for another 24 hours
options.rationale: Aspirin dose adjustment does not address IVIG resistance; second IVIG or corticosteroid is required to reduce ongoing vasculitis and aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically targeting toxic shock syndrome and septicaemia
options.rationale: Cultures should be drawn but the clinical picture meets Kawasaki criteria with documented coronary changes; antibiotics alone do not treat the underlying vasculitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Administer a second dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g per kg and add intravenous methylprednisolone
options.rationale: IVIG resistance is defined as persistent or recurrent fever 36 hours after the first IVIG dose; the AU recommendation is a second IVIG dose at 2 g per kg with methylprednisolone, given the documented coronary involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Switch aspirin to clopidogrel and commence oral prednisolone alone as a steroid-sparing maintenance regimen
options.rationale: Clopidogrel monotherapy is not appropriate and oral prednisolone alone does not match the second-line evidence base for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki with coronary involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis with peak incidence in children under 5; untreated, around a quarter develop coronary artery aneurysms. The first-line therapy is intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g per kg given within day 10 of fever combined with aspirin. IVIG resistance, defined as persistent or recrudescent fever 36 hours after IVIG, occurs in 10 to 20% and significantly increases aneurysm risk; AU guidance recommends a second IVIG dose plus pulsed methylprednisolone in this scenario, with infliximab as third-line. Echocardiographic surveillance is repeated at 2 and 6 weeks regardless of initial findings.
key_points: IVIG resistance = persistent or recurrent fever 36 hours after first IVIG
Coronary artery involvement at baseline raises the threshold for prompt second-line therapy
Second-line = repeat IVIG 2 g/kg plus IV methylprednisolone in AU
Infliximab is reserved for third-line when steroids and repeat IVIG fail
explanation.pearls: Persistent fever beyond 36 hours after IVIG 2 g per kg in Kawasaki defines resistance and warrants repeat IVIG plus IV methylprednisolone in Australia.

stem: A 9-year-old girl with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes presents with 48 hours of polyuria, vomiting, abdominal pain, and rapid breathing. She is alert with sunken eyes and a dry tongue. Her venous blood gas shows pH 7.10, HCO3 8 mmol/L, glucose 28 mmol/L, blood ketones 5.6 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L. She weighs 30 kg. She has had no recent fluid intake. Her initial 10 mL per kg normal saline bolus has been completed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 134, BP 96/58, RR 36 (Kussmaul), SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Alert; capillary refill 3 seconds; dry mucous membranes; mild abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 28, pH 7.10, HCO3 8, ketones 5.6, K+ 4.2, Na+ 138; BSL on point-of-care confirms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate fluid and insulin plan for the next phase?

options.text: Continue saline boluses at 20 mL per kg per hour until the heart rate normalises, then start insulin at 0.1 units per kg per hour
options.rationale: Aggressive bolusing beyond initial resuscitation increases cerebral oedema risk in paediatric DKA; replacement fluid is calculated and given more slowly. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous insulin infusion 0.1 units per kg per hour immediately before any further fluid replacement
options.rationale: Insulin is started 1 to 2 hours after fluids in paediatric DKA to allow for circulating volume restoration and to reduce the risk of cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol per kg over 1 hour given the severity of the acidaemia on venous gas
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is associated with worsened cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA and is reserved for severe acidaemia with haemodynamic instability after appropriate fluid resuscitation. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Switch to 5% dextrose with potassium chloride 40 mmol per litre immediately and titrate to capillary blood glucose
options.rationale: Dextrose is added only when blood glucose falls below 14 to 17 mmol/L; switching prematurely worsens hyperglycaemia and delays acidosis correction. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Replace a 5 to 7% deficit over 48 hours with 0.9% saline plus potassium and start insulin 0.05 to 0.1 units per kg per hour at 1 hour
options.rationale: AU paediatric DKA guidance replaces an assumed deficit (5 to 7% for moderate, up to 10% for severe) over 48 hours alongside maintenance using saline with potassium once urine output established; insulin starts at 0.05 to 0.1 units per kg per hour after 1 hour to reduce cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG/ISPAD Paediatric DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA management balances correction of dehydration and acidosis against the catastrophic risk of cerebral oedema, which carries 20 to 25% mortality. Fluid resuscitation beyond an initial 10 mL per kg bolus is gradual: total deficit plus maintenance over 48 hours, with frequent neurological observations. Insulin is started after at least 1 hour of fluids at a low rate; bicarbonate, rapid sodium swings, and excessive fluid administration are independent risk factors for cerebral oedema. Dextrose is added when glucose falls toward 14 to 17 mmol/L to allow ongoing insulin infusion without hypoglycaemia.
key_points: Initial bolus 10 mL/kg only unless shocked; further boluses raise cerebral oedema risk
Deficit replaced over 48 hours, not 24, in paediatric DKA
Insulin 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/h started 1 hour after fluids
Bicarbonate is generally avoided
explanation.pearls: AU paediatric DKA: 5 to 7% deficit replaced over 48 hours with saline+KCl; insulin 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/h started after 1 hour, bicarbonate avoided.

stem: A 48-year-old woman attends a general practice appointment requesting help to quit smoking. She has smoked 25 cigarettes a day for 28 years and has tried lozenges twice in the past with relapse within 4 weeks. She is keen to set a quit date in the next fortnight and is open to medication. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on perindopril and a remote episode of moderate depression treated with sertraline 6 years ago, now off all psychotropics. She is not pregnant, does not drink alcohol heavily, and has no history of seizures.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 132/82, RR 14, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: PHQ-9 today 4; engaged, motivated; no current suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: Exhaled carbon monoxide 22 ppm; eGFR 88; LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy to support her quit attempt?

options.text: Commence varenicline titrated over 7 days, starting 1 to 2 weeks before her chosen quit date, for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation in Australia and is first-line for motivated patients without contraindications; titration starts up to 2 weeks before the quit date. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Prescribe combination nicotine replacement therapy with a patch plus oral form for 12 weeks from quit date
options.rationale: Combination NRT is effective and reasonable but offers slightly lower abstinence rates than varenicline in head-to-head AU comparisons; it remains a second-line first choice for those preferring NRT. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Prescribe bupropion 150 mg daily for 3 days then twice daily for 7 to 12 weeks starting before her quit date
options.rationale: Bupropion is an option but has more drug interactions and a small seizure risk; it is not first-line over varenicline or combination NRT in current AU guidance. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Recommend e-cigarettes via a prescription model from her general practitioner for 12 weeks alongside counselling
options.rationale: Nicotine vaping is now prescription-only in Australia and considered second-line after first-line therapies have failed or been declined. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Defer pharmacotherapy and provide brief advice with referral to Quitline only as initial behavioural support
options.rationale: She has failed two attempts with monotherapy NRT lozenges; behavioural support alone is unlikely to succeed and should be combined with effective pharmacotherapy. (Source: RACGP: Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: RACGP guidance positions varenicline and combination NRT as first-line pharmacotherapies for smoking cessation in adults, with bupropion as an effective alternative when those are unsuitable. Combining pharmacotherapy with behavioural support (Quitline, brief intervention) is more effective than either alone. Varenicline now carries a more cautious rather than absolute mental health warning following large trials showing no excess neuropsychiatric events, but patients with current major depression warrant closer monitoring. Nicotine vaping products require a doctor prescription and are considered after first-line failure or refusal.
key_points: Varenicline and combination NRT are first-line in Australia
Bupropion is second-line and contraindicated with seizure risk
Behavioural support combined with pharmacotherapy improves quit rates
Vaping is prescription-only and used after first-line failure
explanation.pearls: Varenicline: start titration 1 to 2 weeks before quit date and continue for 12 weeks total per RACGP guidance.

stem: A 26-year-old man with a 9-month history of first-episode schizophrenia is reviewed by the early-psychosis team. He has been taking olanzapine 15 mg nightly for 7 months and is now in symptomatic remission. Since starting treatment his weight has increased from 78 kg to 92 kg and his waist circumference from 92 cm to 108 cm. He is otherwise well, walks his dog daily, and would prefer to stay on olanzapine because his mental state is settled. He has no chest pain, no thirst, and no polyuria. His family history includes type 2 diabetes in both parents.

data_table.Examination: Mental state stable; no involuntary movements; no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: Random glucose 5.8 mmol/L; HbA1c 5.7%; LDL 4.2 mmol/L; triglycerides 2.4 mmol/L; ALT 56

lead_in: Which monitoring and management step is most appropriate at this point?

options.text: Continue olanzapine unchanged and reassess metabolic parameters in 12 months given his clinical stability
options.rationale: Annual review is insufficient once weight and lipid changes are emerging; AU early-psychosis guidance recommends a structured response when waist or weight thresholds are exceeded. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Refer to dietitian and exercise programme, repeat fasting lipids and glucose, and consider switching to aripiprazole or amisulpride
options.rationale: Weight gain over 7% from baseline, central adiposity and dyslipidaemia trigger lifestyle intervention plus consideration of antipsychotic switch to a lower metabolic burden agent such as aripiprazole or amisulpride. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg twice daily to attenuate olanzapine-associated weight gain and ignore lipid abnormalities
options.rationale: Metformin can attenuate weight gain in selected patients but does not address dyslipidaemia and is not the first step alone; lifestyle and switch consideration are preferred. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Switch immediately to clozapine given the magnitude of metabolic change and his early stability on olanzapine
options.rationale: Clozapine has a higher metabolic burden than olanzapine and is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after two adequate trials. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine to 7.5 mg, add a statin, and avoid switching to minimise relapse risk in early remission
options.rationale: Dose reduction below the effective therapeutic range risks relapse; adding statin treats one element only and does not address weight or glycaemic risk. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotics, particularly olanzapine and clozapine, carry a significant metabolic burden through weight gain, dyslipidaemia and impaired glucose tolerance. AU monitoring frameworks track weight, waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and lipids at baseline, at 6 weeks, at 3 months, then at least annually. A weight increase of more than 7% from baseline or a worsening lipid profile prompts lifestyle intervention and consideration of switching to a lower-burden agent such as aripiprazole or amisulpride. Clozapine is preserved for treatment-resistant cases.
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic burden
Switching to aripiprazole or amisulpride attenuates weight gain
Monitoring at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months, then annually is the AU standard
Metformin can be added when switching is undesirable or insufficient
explanation.pearls: A 7% rise in weight from baseline on an antipsychotic triggers RANZCP recommended intervention with lifestyle, monitoring and consideration of switching to aripiprazole or amisulpride.

stem:
A 64-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with 24 hours of rigors and confusion. He has a history of type 2 diabetes on metformin and a recent dental abscess treated with oral amoxicillin 3 days ago. He is now disoriented to time. The triage nurse documents a quick SOFA score of 3 (altered mentation, RR 28, BP 88/50).

Venous blood gas shows lactate 4.1 mmol/L. Blood cultures have been drawn through a peripheral cannula. No imaging has been performed yet and no source of infection beyond the dental history is clear on initial examination.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, BP 88/50, RR 28, SpO2 95% room air, Temp 39.0
data_table.Examination: Confused, dry mucous membranes; mild tenderness over right mandible; no rash; clear chest

lead_in: What is the single most important next action within the hour-1 bundle?

options.text: Order a contrast-enhanced CT of the head, chest, abdomen and pelvis to identify the source before antibiotic administration
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay antibiotics in septic shock; the source is suggested by recent dental abscess, and antibiotics plus fluids are the priority within the hour-1 bundle. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Insert a central venous catheter for noradrenaline before any antibiotic administration to avoid extravasation
options.rationale: Noradrenaline can be commenced peripherally short-term while access is upgraded; delaying antibiotics for central access worsens outcome. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, give a 30 mL per kg crystalloid bolus, and start vasopressors if MAP remains below 65
options.rationale: The hour-1 bundle requires lactate measurement, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL per kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4, and vasopressors if MAP remains under 65 mmHg. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Repeat blood cultures from a second site before any antibiotic administration to maximise culture yield
options.rationale: Two sets from separate sites is ideal but should not delay antibiotics in septic shock; the goal is administration within the hour. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Administer 100 mg of hydrocortisone immediately as the priority over fluid resuscitation and antibiotic delivery
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is added when shock is refractory to adequate fluid and vasopressor therapy, not before fluids and antibiotics. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

explanation.summary: Septic shock has a time-dependent mortality and the international hour-1 bundle is operationalised in Australia through Therapeutic Guidelines. Within the first hour clinicians should obtain lactate, draw blood cultures (without delaying antibiotics), administer broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting likely source, and resuscitate hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol per L with 30 mL per kg of crystalloid. Vasopressors (noradrenaline first-line) are added if MAP remains under 65 mmHg after fluids. Source identification and source control follow as resuscitation proceeds.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors if MAP <65 after fluids
Initial crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate >4
Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor
Source control is parallel to, not before, resuscitation
explanation.pearls: Lactate above 4 mmol/L or MAP below 65 mmHg defines the threshold for full hour-1 resuscitation including 30 mL/kg crystalloid and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics.

stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents with her father to the general practitioner with 8 weeks of large, painful, hard bowel motions occurring once every 5 to 7 days. She has been soiling her underwear with small amounts of soft stool most days. She is otherwise well, fully toilet-trained for urine, and started prep this year. Her diet is varied but limited in fluids.

She has had no abdominal distension since infancy, normal growth, and no delayed passage of meconium. Abdominal examination reveals a palpable faecal mass in the left iliac fossa with no other findings.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with palpable left iliac fossa faecal mass; no perianal abnormality; growth on 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer to a paediatric gastroenterologist for rectal manometry to exclude Hirschsprung disease before any therapy
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease typically presents in infancy with delayed meconium and abdominal distension; manometry referral is not first-line for a 5-year-old with classic functional constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Recommend dietary fibre, increased fluids, and a daily fruit-juice trial without any laxatives initially
options.rationale: Diet alone is insufficient when impaction is present and overflow soiling is occurring; pharmacological disimpaction is required first. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Prescribe a single phosphate enema to disimpact and then review need for ongoing oral laxative therapy
options.rationale: Oral macrogol is preferred over enemas in children because of acceptability and equal efficacy; phosphate enemas have a small risk of electrolyte disturbance. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Commence oral macrogol disimpaction regimen with escalating daily doses over 3 to 7 days, then move to a maintenance dose
options.rationale: Functional constipation with faecal impaction and overflow soiling is treated with oral macrogol disimpaction over 3 to 7 days followed by maintenance dosing, alongside toileting and behavioural advice. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Start senna 7.5 mg nightly for 6 weeks without disimpaction phase and review at the end of the course
options.rationale: Stimulant laxatives are used in addition to macrogol when response is poor; they are not first-line monotherapy and skipping disimpaction perpetuates overflow. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation accounts for over 90% of paediatric chronic constipation and is managed with a disimpaction phase followed by maintenance. AU paediatric guidance recommends oral macrogol with electrolytes as first-line for both phases; disimpaction uses escalating daily doses over 3 to 7 days then steps down to a maintenance dose titrated to one soft motion per day. Behavioural toileting after meals (using the gastrocolic reflex) and stool diaries support adherence. Red flags for organic causes (delayed meconium, failure to thrive, abdominal distension) prompt specialist referral.
key_points: Oral macrogol is first-line for disimpaction and maintenance
Overflow soiling indicates impaction and mandates disimpaction first
Senna is added as a second agent if macrogol alone is insufficient
Red flags for Hirschsprung include delayed meconium and infantile onset
explanation.pearls: Macrogol disimpaction at escalating daily doses over 3 to 7 days followed by maintenance dosing is the AU first-line paediatric constipation strategy.

stem:
A 16-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her mother after the mother found a written plan to take 30 paracetamol tablets the next day, alongside the cached tablets in her bedroom. The young woman is currently calm, articulate, and minimises the plan, saying it was a moment of frustration after an argument. She has been treated for moderate depression by her general practitioner for 4 months with limited response to sertraline 100 mg daily.

She lives with her mother who is supportive; her father has limited contact. She has had two emergency presentations in the past 6 months for superficial cutting; nothing has been ingested today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 110/68, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Composed; mood flat; no thought disorder; HEADSSS reveals social withdrawal and disordered sleep
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated; paracetamol level not relevant (no ingestion); urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition plan?

options.text: Discharge home with safety planning, mother as primary supervisor, and outpatient psychology follow-up within 7 days
options.rationale: Active suicide plan with method, cached tablets, and limited treatment response is not adequately mitigated by community safety planning alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents

options.text: Refer to school-based counselling and increase sertraline to 150 mg daily without acute admission
options.rationale: Increasing antidepressant dose without acute assessment misses the urgency of a planned ingestion and provides inadequate immediate safety. (Source: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents

options.text: Admit only if she expresses ongoing intent within the next 2 hours during repeat interview by the registrar
options.rationale: Adolescents often minimise on direct questioning; basing admission on second-interview disclosure underestimates risk after a concrete plan with cached means. (Source: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents

options.text: Discharge under a verbal contract of safety with the mother removing tablets at home and a phone call from CYMHS in 72 hours
options.rationale: Verbal contracts of safety lack evidence and are no longer recommended; phone follow-up after 72 hours does not address acute risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents

options.text: Admit to a paediatric ward for safety, urgent child and youth mental health service review, and means restriction at home
options.rationale: Concrete plan, cached means, repeated self-harm and inadequate antidepressant response warrant inpatient admission for safety and urgent CYMHS review with collaborative means restriction planning before discharge. (Source: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents

explanation.summary: Adolescent self-harm and suicidality is assessed across intent, plan, means, prior attempts, mental disorder, and protective factors. A specific plan with cached means, particularly paracetamol, and prior self-harm with limited treatment response confers high acute risk that exceeds community management. AU paediatric guidance supports inpatient admission with urgent specialist review (CAMHS/CYMHS) and concrete means-restriction. Safety contracts have no evidence base and have been replaced by collaborative safety planning involving the young person, family, and clinical team.
key_points: Cached method (especially paracetamol) raises acute risk substantially
Verbal safety contracts have no evidence and are not used
Inpatient admission is appropriate for high-risk plans with limited community supports
Means restriction is a core part of every disposition
explanation.pearls: Cached paracetamol with a written plan in an adolescent with prior self-harm meets RCH criteria for inpatient admission and urgent CYMHS review.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Self-harm in Children and Adolescents
